results.possibleIdentity: Frank Kuenstler (1928–1996) was an American avant-garde poet, experimental filmmaker, and assemblage artist active primarily in New York City from the 1950s through the 1990s. He was a central figure in the mid-1960s New York literary and artistic scene, notably as a founding member of 'The Eventorium,' an arts collective and gallery on Manhattan's Upper West Side. Kuenstler is best known for his iconoclastic book-length poem 'LENS' (1964), which was published by Jonas Mekas's 'Film Culture' and is characterized by a radical, compressed syntax of word pairs. His work is often associated with the precursor movements to 'Language' poetry and Fluxus; he is listed in historical records alongside participants of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) circle and the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' event. As a filmmaker, he produced experimental works such as 'Color Idioms' (1968) and 'El Atlantis' (1973), the latter featuring a soundtrack played by Kuenstler on a toy piano. He also created 'Mauerskulpturen' (wall sculptures) consisting of wood assemblages from found objects. Kuenstler taught at the School of Visual Arts in New York and edited magazines such as 'Bread&' and 'Airplane.'

results.possibleIdentity: Harley Jones was a filmmaker, artist, and educator active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his collaboration with the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), specifically for his work in documenting their major projects. Jones, along with Alan Taylor, produced and directed the film 'The Pepsi Pavilion' (1970), which documented the landmark multisensory environment created by E.A.T. for the 1970 World Exposition (Expo '70) in Osaka, Japan. In the late 1960s, Jones was a staff member and lecturer in film-making at the Newport College of Art (then Newport Monmouthshire College of Art) in Wales. His name appears in the context of the avant-garde film community, notably mentioned in the correspondence of filmmaker Bruce Baillie regarding Canyon Cinema and international film education. In the mid-1970s, he was also associated with the Arts Council of Great Britain, serving on various committees related to the arts and film.
